stem: In a legal outcomes study comparing Judge A vs. Judge B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acquittal rate (58.6% vs 52.9%). However, when stratified by case severity: In misdemeanors (n_X=40, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 77% vs Treatment Y's 68%. In felonies (n_X=100, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 43% vs Treatment Y's 36%. The true population proportion of misdemeanors is uncertain, estimated between 45% and 64%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to felonies (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.079, 0.083], point estimate: 0.081. Treatment X is genuinely better by 8.1 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=52.9%, Y=58.6% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In misdemeanors: X=77% > Y=68%. In felonies: X=43% > Y=36%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Judge A is applied more to felonies (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
Not considering that additional unobserved confounders could further modify the comparison
